questions.prompt: Which PSE menu section would you open to listen back to a specific call's recording?
questions.explanation: Recordings live under Reports and Recordings (alongside CDR). The fastest path is finding the call in CDR (Advanced), clicking it, and using the in-page playback; the Recordings tab also lets you browse all recordings independent of a CDR row.

questions.prompt: Which extension-profile tab tells you which Linkus clients are currently logged in for that user?
questions.explanation: The Linkus Clients tab on the extension profile lists every registered client (Desktop / Web / Mobile) with last-seen timestamps. Most 'why isn't my Linkus working?' tickets resolve right there.

questions.prompt: Which CDR fields are most useful when triaging an inbound call that 'went to the wrong place'? (Select all that apply.)
choices: DID/DDI (the dialled number — tells you which inbound route matched)
Last Participant (where the call ended up)
questions.explanation: DID, Source Trunk, and Last Participant together tell you the routing path. PIN Code is for restricted outbound routes (not inbound). Caller IP is media-side info, not routing info.

questions.prompt: On PSE firmware 83.21.0.117 or later, both 'CDR (Advanced)' and 'CDR (Legacy)' tabs can have data; new calls land in Advanced, older calls remain in Legacy until cleanup ages them out.
questions.explanation: The 83.21.0.117 release introduced a new CDR data structure. Older records keep living under the Legacy tab until the auto-cleanup setting purges them. When a customer upgrades, expect both tabs to be populated for a while.

questions.prompt: What does Timeline View (in CDR detail) show that Standard View doesn't?
choices: Chronological per-event listing of everything that happened during the call (entered IVR, transferred to queue, hold start/end, hangup) — call lifecycle granularity
questions.explanation: Timeline View gives chronological events through the call's lifecycle. Standard View is the flat summary. Timeline View needs the 'call timeline' feature enabled in PBX Settings → Preferences; if not enabled, Standard View is all you get.

questions.prompt: A customer asks 'why was this call recorded?'. You read the recording rules in Call Features → Recording. Each rule matches calls by which combination of attributes?
choices: Call type (inbound / outbound / internal) plus targets (extensions / trunks / queues) plus enable state
questions.explanation: Recording rules combine call type, target sets, and an enabled flag. A rule matching 'inbound calls to the Sales queue' records every inbound call that hit Sales regardless of who answered. Knowing this lets you read why-or-why-not without changing the rule.

questions.prompt: When you download a call recording from the PSE admin portal, the operation is logged in Maintenance → Operation Logs with your username, the call ID, and the timestamp.
questions.explanation: Recording downloads are audited. The Operation Logs page is the audit trail customers can review. This is why you should never share recordings outside that trail — emailing a copy from your personal address bypasses the log.

questions.prompt: Sarah dials an external number and her CDR row shows Status = FAILED with Last Participant indicating 'No matching route'. What's the most likely cause?
choices: The outbound route does not have a number pattern that matches the dialled number, so PSE has nowhere to send the call
questions.explanation: 'No matching route' is PSE's way of saying the outbound routes evaluated for that extension don't have a number pattern that catches the dialled number. The fix is in Call Control → Outbound Routes: either Sarah's role lacks access to a route that would catch it, or the pattern is wrong / missing.

questions.prompt: You're triaging a 'connected but no audio' ticket. The SIP trace shows INVITE → 200 OK → ACK normally. The 200 OK's SDP body has `c=10.0.5.42` and `m=audio 21000 RTP/AVP 8`. The trunk is on the public internet. What's the most likely root cause?
choices: The SDP is advertising a private RFC 1918 IP that the trunk side can't reach for RTP; NAT settings on the PBX SIP profile probably need adjusting
questions.explanation: 10.0.5.42 is RFC 1918 (10.x). A public-internet trunk can't route RTP to a private address. The fix is per-trunk or per-extension NAT settings in the SIP profile so PSE rewrites the SDP with the public IP. PCMA (payload type 8) is fine.

questions.prompt: Put these triage-data items in the order a useful escalation would present them:
items: Affected extension number and the precise time range
CDR row screenshot or row ID with the headline Status
Timeline View / SIP trace showing the failure point
Suspected root cause based on the gathered data
questions.explanation: Symptom → context (who, when) → data (CDR) → detail (Timeline/trace) → hypothesis. This lets the next tier read top-to-bottom and stop reading as soon as they have what they need.

questions.prompt: A misconfigured timezone on the PBX (PBX Settings → Date and Time) can cause business-hours time conditions to fire at the wrong wall-clock times, sending calls to wrong destinations.
questions.explanation: Classic callflow gotcha. Time conditions are evaluated against PSE's local time; if the timezone is wrong, the 'business hours' window is offset by hours from reality. Always sanity-check the timezone when 'wrong destination at specific times' tickets come in.

questions.prompt: A triage tech's default action when investigating a ticket is to *read* PSE rather than *change* it; most callflow / extension / role tickets resolve without any configuration changes by the triage tier.
questions.explanation: Most tickets resolve at the read step. Changes to extensions, callflows, recording rules, and roles have downstream effects that aren't always visible from the symptom. Read first; escalate cleanly when the fix is genuinely an intermediate / advanced course's responsibility.
